topic: Strategic Methods: How to Pursue Strategies
subject: business-studies

items.q: Do most mergers and takeovers deliver the benefits promised?
items.correct: Many fail to — culture clashes, overpaying for the target and integration problems often erode the expected synergies, which is why careful evaluation matters.
items.misconception: Combining two successful firms automatically creates a stronger one.
items.random_wrong: Mergers are banned under UK law.
items.why: Students assume scale automatically equals success; the synergy trap is a key evaluative point in strategic methods.

items.q: Is organic growth always slower and worse than takeover-led growth?
items.correct: It is usually slower, but it is lower-risk, preserves the firm's culture and avoids integration costs; which is better depends on objectives and market conditions.
items.misconception: Buying another company is always the best route to growth.
items.random_wrong: Organic growth refers only to companies selling organic food.
items.why: The literal misreading of 'organic' is common, and students miss the risk-speed trade-off between growth methods.

items.q: Does innovation only mean inventing brand-new products?
items.correct: No — process innovation, improving how things are made or delivered, is equally important and often gives bigger cost and quality gains than new products.
items.misconception: Only firms launching new gadgets count as innovative.
items.random_wrong: Innovation is restricted to firms holding a government licence.
items.why: Students narrow innovation to invention, missing process, marketing and business-model innovation.

items.q: A merger differs from a takeover because:
items.correct: The two businesses agree to combine into one joint organisation
items.misconception: One business buys a controlling interest in the other
items.random_wrong: It can only happen between firms in different countries
items.why: Correct answer: The two businesses agree to combine into one joint organisation. “One business buys a controlling interest in the other” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Backward vertical integration is when a business joins with:
items.correct: A supplier earlier in its supply chain
items.misconception: A retailer closer to the final customer
items.random_wrong: A direct competitor at the same stage
items.why: Correct answer: A supplier earlier in its supply chain. “A retailer closer to the final customer” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: Grows too quickly without enough working capital to fund the growth
items.misconception: Sells goods abroad without an export licence
items.random_wrong: Holds too much cash in reserve
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Grows too quickly without enough working capital to fund the growth. “Sells goods abroad without an export licence” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: A new invention or process, for a fixed period
items.random_wrong: An original piece of writing or music
items.why: Correct answer: A new invention or process, for a fixed period. “A brand name or logo” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A takeover creates synergies cutting combined annual costs of £50 million by 4%. What is the annual saving in £ million?
items.why: The real figure is 2 £ million. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
